## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: HIGH.** This is the dossier's strongest positive.

- The **€6bn CASA 2025 net-income target, set in the 2022 MTP, was hit one year early** (already at €6bn 9M-2025 net income in Q3-2025) — and management hit its full prior-MTP targets early **three years running** (stated Q4-2024, corroborated by the EPS step from 1.94→2.11→2.18).
- The screener's **83% beat rate (5/6 qtrs)** corroborates the transcript claim of consistent delivery.
- **Sandbagging pattern: YES, mild and consistent.** L'Angevin guided LCL NII "stable in 2025" (Q2) while the underlying was already inflecting positively, then French-retail NII printed +11% in Q4 — she then guided 2026 to "high single-digit," explicitly calling the +11% "probably a little bit strong." This is a team that under-promises on NII and lets it beat. Good for credibility, but it means the upside is partly *already in management's own conservative guide*, limiting surprise.
- **The one credibility test for the future:** ACT 2028 is the first plan where the bar is set by *this* CEO (Gavalda) and CFO (L'Angevin). The prior three-in-a-row early beats were Grivet-era. The track record transfers to the institution, but the new principals are unproven over a full cycle.

**Score: HIGH** (4–5/5 on delivery).

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

Recurring themes analysts pushed on:

1. **French-retail / LCL NIM inflection (every call).** Asked repeatedly when the bottom is in. Management was honest that it was delayed for the Regional Banks, then delivered. This one resolved *favorably* — a case where analyst worry did NOT turn into bad news.
2. **The share-price underperformance vs European bank peers despite beating guidance (Q3-2025, Kumar/DB explicitly; "is there a message to be taken here?").** This is the *crux*. Management's only answer is "regularity is a strong point" — i.e. **they have no catalyst for the valuation discount to close.** The market has watched CASA out-deliver and still de-rate it relative to peers. This is the single most important unaddressed analyst concern.
3. **Banco BPM accounting volatility (Q2, Q3, Q4 repeatedly).** Analysts struggled to model the moving pieces (fair-value-through-P&L, OCI, equity-method transition, €607m first-consolidation). Management guidance shifted ("strong negative impact" → "several million negative" → resolved on consolidation). Not deception, but a genuine modelling headache that injected quarterly noise and likely contributed to the discount.
4. **French fiscal / tax surcharge & political risk (Q1, Q3, Q4).** Recurring. Management consistently flags the corporate-tax surcharge (€147m CASA FY-2025) and "we always include buffers" for French/Italian fiscal uncertainty. Honest, but an open-ended overhang.
5. **UK motor-finance litigation (Q4-2024 first raised, Q3-2025 €20m provision).** Management downplays ("not going to be a major issue… loss-sharing with Stellantis from the FCA-Bank JV era"). A small but live tail that analysts correctly flagged early.
6. **Interim dividend / buybacks (Q1-2025, Q3-2025).** Analysts probing for capital-return upside. Management is "agnostic," explicitly states **buybacks are NOT part of the usual distribution strategy** (cash dividend only; the small Q4 buyback is just anti-dilution for employee shares). This **closes off the most obvious re-rating lever** a value investor would hope for.

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🟢 **Fortress solvency & liquidity** — CET1 17.1% group (6.7pp buffer), liquidity reserves €488bn, funding plan over-achieved at favourable spreads (Q4-2025). Best-in-class.
🟢 **Genuine delivery culture** — 2025 target hit a year early, third year running; 83% beat rate corroborated.
🟢 **NIM inflection delivered** — French-retail NII +11% Q4-2025; the multi-year headwind turned to tailwind exactly as guided (just later for Regional Banks).
🟢 **Diversified, counter-cyclical mix** — insurance (record €52bn premium, +20%) and Amundi (€88bn inflows, ×1.6) cushion the thin-spread retail book; fee income now >50% of French-retail NDI.
🟢 **Conservative, sandbagged guidance** — under-promises NII, builds fiscal buffers.

🔴 **Net income has plateaued** — flat ~€7.1bn FY-2024→FY-2025; ACT-2028 implies only ~2%/yr bottom-line CAGR to >€8.5bn. A low-growth compounder dressed as a "conquering bank."
🔴 **Rising cost/income through 2025** (53.9%→55.7%) — management calls it a "peak" and promises improvement, but it is a *promise*, not a result; integration/investment costs are real and ongoing.
🔴 **Cost of risk drifting up** — CASA CoR 26→35bps over the window; attributed to IFRS-9 models + SFS/consumer + UK motor finance. Plausible but the consumer-finance/Agos book and auto-residual-value risk (EVs, China JV) are a soft spot.
🔴 **No buyback, by policy** — the single most powerful re-rating lever for a sub-book, high-payout bank is explicitly off the table.
🔴 **Persistent peer-relative de-rating despite out-delivery** — management has no answer for why the discount should close; structural conglomerate complexity (insurance + CIB + agri + the Regional-Banks cross-guarantee + Banco BPM optics) is the likely cause and is not being simplified.
🔴 **French sovereign / fiscal overhang** — OAT-swap spreads, recurring tax surcharges, banking-fee proposals in parliament; CASA's funding and tax base are France-concentrated.
🔴 **Serial-acquirer dilution risk** — a "dozen operations" per year (Milleis, Thaler, Crelan, CACEIS, Banco BPM, ICG, Victory) keeps goodwill rising and the RoE-vs-RoTE wedge wide; integration is a permanent line item.

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **Terminology shift toward "partnership + minority stake" as a strategy.** By Q2-2025 L'Angevin openly frames the Banco BPM playbook (own >20%, equity-account, lock in 2034/2043 distribution partnerships) as a *replicable template* (Victory 26%, Crelan <10%, GAC JV). This is a "scale-via-affiliation" model that avoids full-control capital strain but **embeds CASA's earnings inside stakes it doesn't fully consolidate** — opaque, and a driver of the conglomerate discount.
- **The pre-CMD "Excel housekeeping" (Q3-2025)** — simplifying RoTE methodology and OCI retreatment "to bring it perfectly close to market standards" — is a tell that management *knows* its reporting complexity has been hurting the multiple and is trying to fix the optics. A subtle admission that the discount is partly self-inflicted.
- **Worldline stake (Q2-2025):** when Worldline's market value collapsed, management reflexively minimised ("7%… fair-value-through-OCI… impact very limited… what matters is the industrial partnership"). The speed of the deflection signals sensitivity to the "CASA holds a grab-bag of impaired minority stakes" critique.
- **New CEO Gavalda's vocabulary** ("conquering bank," "leader in transitions," "leader in new technologies," tokenized finance, AI) is markedly more aspirational/growth-coded than Grivet's measured CFO register — a deliberate repositioning of the equity story toward "growth/quality" to escape the "cheap French bank" box. Whether substance follows is the open question.
- **The Regional Banks' own €350m buyback (Q2-2025)** — done at the *mutual* level, not CASA — underscores that capital-return creativity exists in the Group but is structurally walled off from the listed entity.

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: STABLE (high-quality, low-growth compounder), with a PIVOTING veneer.**

The underlying franchise is genuinely strong, fortress-capitalised, and delivers exactly what it promises — a ~€7bn, ~13.5% RoTE, 6.8%-yield European universal bank that hit its last plan a year early three times running. The fundamentals are *not* deteriorating. But neither are they inflecting upward: net income has plateaued, C/I and CoR ticked the wrong way in 2025, growth is bolt-on-and-partnership financial engineering rather than organic operating leverage, and the bottom-line ambition (>€8.5bn by 2028 ≈ 2%/yr) is modest. The "conquering bank / ACT 2028" framing is a *narrative* pivot to shake off the cheap-bank label, not an operational one.

**The single most important thing the director must weigh before sizing:** *the catalyst is already spent.* The thesis here is pure relative-value (EPV/Graham/OwnerEarnings MoS of +53–79% vs an already-cheap peer set) — but the one dated event that could have closed that discount, the **18-Nov-2025 ACT-2028 Capital Markets Day, has already happened** and produced only a constructive-not-explosive analyst reception (UBS reiterate Buy, PT €18) and a **flat share price** (~€16.5 then and now). The **2025 dividend (€1.13) has already gone ex (27-May-2026) and been paid.** Management has **explicitly ruled out buybacks**, removing the obvious re-rating lever. So the director is being asked to own a cheap, high-quality, dividend-paying conglomerate whose discount is structural (complexity + French risk) and whose near-term re-rating catalysts are in the rear-view mirror. This is a *carry-and-hold-for-the-dividend-and-slow-compounding* position, **not** an event-driven special situation — and it must be sized as the former.
